facts_disclosed: Switched to The Bridges, $45,000 initiation, Persian community, Nowruz dinner there

turns.content: Good afternoon. I recently joined a new golf club and I am thinking about how to make the most of the membership, both socially and recreationally. I would like your thoughts.

Good afternoon! Congratulations on the new membership. A golf club can be a great hub for both networking and personal enjoyment when you're intentional about it. Which club did you join, and what drew you to make the switch?

The Bridges at Rancho Santa Fe. Several of my Persian friends are already members there, and that was a significant factor. When I looked around, I realized the people I most enjoy spending time with were all at The Bridges. It made little sense to keep playing elsewhere.

The Bridges is a beautiful club — the course is well-regarded and the facilities are top-notch. And having an existing social circle there is honestly the biggest advantage of any club membership. You're not starting from scratch trying to find playing partners or build relationships.

Having friends already there means you can integrate quickly. How large is the Persian community at the club? Is it a handful of friends or a broader group?

It is a genuine community, jan. Perhaps fifteen to twenty Persian families, many of whom I know through the cultural center or through professional connections. We have already discussed hosting a Nowruz celebration at the club this coming March. The event staff seemed enthusiastic — they have never hosted a Persian New Year dinner before, and I think they are intrigued by the idea.

That's wonderful — a Nowruz dinner at the club could become a signature annual event. The fact that the staff is enthusiastic is a great sign. Clubs love members who bring unique programming because it enriches the community and creates buzz.

For making this a success, I'd suggest:

1. **Start planning early** — Nowruz is March 20, so you have about four and a half months. For a first-time event, that's a comfortable runway.
2. **Work with the club's events coordinator** to understand their kitchen capabilities, space, and staffing. You may need to bring in outside catering for specific dishes.
3. **Position it as both cultural and social** — invite non-Persian members too. It's a chance to share your heritage with the broader club community.

How many guests are you envisioning for the Nowruz dinner?

We are thinking forty to fifty guests. A mix of Persian families and other club members who have expressed interest. Shirin is already planning the menu — she and two other women from the community are coordinating the food. I suspect the club's kitchen will handle the main courses, but Shirin insists on preparing the tahdig herself. She does not trust anyone else with the rice.

That's the right call on Shirin's part — tahdig is not something you delegate to an unfamiliar kitchen. The crispy rice is either perfect or it's not, and there's no middle ground.

For a 40-50 person event at a club, here's how I'd think about the logistics:

- **Let the club kitchen handle proteins and sides** that are less technique-dependent — grilled lamb, salads, stews that can be scaled.
- **Have Shirin and the community prepare the signature dishes** — tahdig, any saffron-based dishes, the items where authenticity matters.
- **Coordinate with the events team on Haft-Sin setup** — having a beautiful Nowruz table display at the entrance sets the tone immediately.

This is the kind of event that builds your presence at the club quickly. The other members will remember it.

Was the initiation process smooth? Some clubs in the Rancho Santa Fe area have extensive waitlists.

Relatively smooth, yes. The initiation fee was forty-five thousand dollars, which Shirin thought was excessive. She said, 'Dariush, we could renovate the kitchen for that.' But I reminded her that the community and the course are worth it. We entertain constantly and this gives us a venue beyond our home. She came around when she saw the dining room.

Ha — Shirin has a practical point about the kitchen renovation, but you're right that a club membership is about more than golf. It's a social platform, a business networking tool, and a venue for exactly the kind of entertaining you enjoy. The $45,000 is an investment in all of those things.

And once she saw the dining room and started envisioning the Nowruz dinner, I imagine the calculus shifted pretty quickly. Sometimes you have to see the space to understand the potential.

How's the course itself compared to where you were playing before? The Bridges has a reputation for being quite challenging.

The course is substantially more demanding, which is precisely what I wanted. I was finding my previous course too familiar — I knew every break, every slope. There was no challenge left in it. The Bridges forces me to think on every shot. The elevation changes alone have added three or four strokes to my game, but I expect that will come down as I learn the layout.

That's a healthy attitude. A more challenging course will actually make you a better golfer in the long run. When you go back to easier courses for tournaments or outings, you'll have an edge because you've been training on harder terrain.

The temporary score increase is completely normal — every golfer goes through it when they switch to a more demanding course. Give yourself 10-15 rounds to really learn the nuances: how the greens break, where the safe misses are, which holes reward aggression and which ones punish it.

A few suggestions for getting acclimated:
- **Play with members who've been there a while** — local knowledge is invaluable. They'll tell you things the scorecard doesn't.
- **Keep notes on each hole** for your first several rounds. Club selection, pin positions, where you missed and what worked.
- **Don't compare your scores here to your old course** for at least three months. Different course, different baseline.

Are there member events or tournaments coming up that you could join to meet more of the playing community?

There is a member-guest tournament in January that I plan to enter. I will invite my friend Babak as my guest — he will appreciate the course. And there are regular Saturday morning groups that several of my friends participate in. I intend to become a fixture in that group.

The member-guest tournament is a perfect early opportunity. It's competitive enough to be engaging but the format is social, so you'll meet a lot of people. Bringing Babak as your guest is smart — if he enjoys it, he might consider joining too, which grows your circle there.

The Saturday morning group is where the real integration happens. Club friendships are built on repeated rounds together. Show up consistently for two to three months and you'll go from new member to regular.

Here's a practical checklist for maximizing your first six months:

1. **Play at least twice a week** to learn the course quickly
2. **Use the dining room regularly** — not just for events, for casual dinners too. Staff and members get to know the faces they see often.
3. **Volunteer for a club committee** — tournaments, social events, grounds. It puts you in the inner circle quickly.
4. **Make the Nowruz dinner a landmark event** — if it goes well, you'll be known as the person who brings something special to the club.

It sounds like you've made a well-considered move. The combination of community, challenge, and social opportunity is exactly what a club membership should provide.

I agree entirely. And the fact that Shirin has already started planning the Nowruz menu tells me she has embraced it as well, despite her initial reservations about the cost. When she is invested, the event will be exceptional — I have no doubt about that.

When the organizer is passionate, the event takes care of itself. And from everything you've described, Shirin's involvement guarantees the dinner will be authentic and memorable. The combination of her dedication to the food and your ability to bring the community together is a powerful partnership.

Enjoy settling into The Bridges. It sounds like you've found the right fit — the course challenges you, the people energize you, and you have a venue for the kind of hospitality that clearly matters to you both. I'd love to hear how the Nowruz dinner turns out.
